request.question: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?

title: La Leocadia
The Wall of Serpents
The Castle of Iron
Gaspar de la Huerta
The Roaring Trumpet
The Ice Dragon
The Green Magician
Luis Royo
The Mathematics of Magic
Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro
sentences: La Leocadia (Spanish: Doña Leocadia) or The Seductress (Spanish: Una Manola) are names given to a mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), completed sometime between 1819–23, as one of his series of 14 "Black Paintings".
It shows a woman commonly identified as Goya's maid, companion and, most likely lover, Leocadia Weiss.
She is dressed in a dark, almost funeral maja dress, and leans against what is either a mantelpiece or burial mound, as she looks outward at the viewer with a sorrowful expression.

"Leocadia" is one of the final of the "Black Paintings", which he painted in his seventies at a time when he was consumed by political, physical and psychological turmoil, after he fled to the country from his position as court painter in Madrid.
The Wall of Serpents is a fantasy novella by American science fiction and fantasy authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fourth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the June 1953 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Fantasy Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Green Magician", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.
It has also been reprinted in various anthologies and collections, including "Great Short Novels of Adult Fantasy I" (1972), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
The Castle of Iron is the title of a fantasy novella by American authors L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, and of the novel into which it was later expanded by the same authors.
It was the third story (and afterwards the second volume) in their Harold Shea series.
As a 35,000 word novella it was first published in the fantasy magazine "Unknown" for April, 1941.
The revised and expanded novel version was first published in hardcover by Gnome Press in 1950, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1962.
The book has been reprinted by a number of other publishers since its first appearance.
An E-book edition was published by Gollancz's SF Gateway imprint on September 29, 2011 as part of a general release of de Camp's works in electronic form.
The novel has been combined with other books in the series in the omnibus editions "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989) and "" (2007).
It has also been translated into Italian.
Gaspar de la Huerta (1645–1714) was a Spanish artist born at Campillo de Altobuey in Cuenca.

At an early age, seeking instruction in Valencia, he fell into the hands of Jesualda Sanchez, the bustling widow of Pedro Infant, a third-rate painter, who continued on her own account her husband's school for the manufacture of religious pictures.
La Huerta, nevertheless, attained some skill as a draughtsman and colourist, and married the widow's well-dowered daughter.
Working for moderate prices he found abundant employment in the neighbouring churches and convents.
For the Franciscans he painted the "Jubilee of the Porciuncula", and for the Dominicans the picture which long served as a veil to the wondrous image of "Our Lady of the Forsaken".
He died at Valencia in 1714.
The Museum of Valencia possesses a pleasing picture of "Christ and the Virgin enthroned", the latter with the bright complexion peculiar to Valencian beauty.
"The Roaring Trumpet" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The initial story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the May 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with its sequel, "The Mathematics of Magic", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.
The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".
It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".
The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.
The Green Magician is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt.
The fifth story in their Harold Shea series, it was first published in the November 1954 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Beyond Fiction".

It first appeared in book form, together with "The Wall of Serpents", in the collection "Wall of Serpents", issued in hardcover by Avalon Books in 1960; the book has been reissued by a number of other publishers since.

It has also been reprinted in various magazines, anthologies and collections, including "The Dragon" (June–July 1978), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), "Masterpieces of Fantasy and Enchantment" (1988), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Italian and German.
Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.

He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.
"The Mathematics of Magic" is a fantasy novella by American writers L. Sprague de Camp and Fletcher Pratt, the second story in their Harold Shea series.
It was first published in the August 1940 issue of the fantasy pulp magazine "Unknown".

It first appeared in book form, together with the preceding novella, "The Roaring Trumpet", in the collection "The Incomplete Enchanter", issued in hardcover by Henry Holt and Company in 1941, and in paperback by Pyramid Books in 1960.

It has since been reprinted in various collections by numerous other publishers, including "The Compleat Enchanter" (1975), "The Incompleat Enchanter" (1979), "The Complete Compleat Enchanter" (1989), and "" (2007).
It has been translated into Dutch and Italian.
In 2016, the story was shortlisted for the Retro Hugo Award for Best Novella.

Pilgrimage to the Fountain of San Isidro or The Holy Office (Spanish: Peregrinación a la fuente de San Isidro or El Santo Official ) are names given to an oil mural by the Spanish artist Francisco Goya (1746–1828), probably completed between 1821 and 1823.
The mural is one of the fourteen Black Paintings that Goya applied in oil on the plaster walls of his house.
Between 1874 and 1878 the paintings were transferred to canvas supports under the direction of the art restorer of the Museo del Prado, Salvador Martinez Cubells.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Disambiguation for short/ambiguous title tokens:
- If an anchor in summary_1 is a short or potentially ambiguous title token, do not automatically append an entertainment-domain keyword. Only append a plain entertainment disambiguator keyword (for example: film movie album song novel author director) when the original question itself contains explicit entertainment-role words (e.g., film, movie, album, song, directed, author, novella) or when the question explicitly lists multiple title-like tokens. In those cases, include the visible anchor(s) from summary_1 plus the appropriate entertainment keyword to bias retrieval. If the question lacks entertainment cues and does not list multiple titles, do not guess or add an entertainment disambiguator—keep the query anchored to the visible tokens only.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- When the context contains a concise bridge claim about a named entity and at least one passage provides an explicit, identity-aligned statement linking that same named entity (or a clearly indicated alias) to the asserted relation, append exactly one brief corroborating sentence that paraphrases that passage's linking fact and references the corroborating passage. Perform an explicit identity-alignment check (alias or explicit linking); do not treat mere name co-occurrence or a passage that merely mentions the name as sufficient. Do not append this corroboration if the `context` already contains the identical corroborating fact verbatim. Keep this addition short and keep the overall summary compact.
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.
- When the summaries contain both a short single-token alias and, in the later integrated summary (summary_2), a contiguous multi-token full-name literal that explicitly identifies or is explicitly linked to the same person (for example via an identity link or biographical framing in summary_2), prefer and emit that multi-token full-name literal exactly as it appears in summary_2. Do not prefer the longer form unless summary_2 provides an explicit identity link tying the alias to the full-name literal; if no such explicit link is present, follow the base identity-link rules and do not force selection of the longer form.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Who wrote the fantasy novella which was reworked in 2014 with a series of paintings by a Spanish artist born in Olalla in 1954?
hop1_docs: The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

Les Femmes d'Alger | Les Femmes d'Alger (Women of Algiers) is a series of 15 paintings and numerous drawings by the Spanish cubist artist Pablo Picasso.  The series, created in 1954-1955, was inspired by Eugène Delacroix's 1834 painting "The Women of Algiers in their Apartment" (French: "Femmes d'Alger dans leur appartement" ).  The series is one of several painted by Picasso in tribute to artists that he admired.
Jesús Olalla | Jesús "Josu" Olalla Iraeta (born July 15, 1971) is a Spanish handball player who competed in the 1996 Summer Olympics and in the 2000 Summer Olympics.

Chris Martin (artist) | Chris Martin (born 1954) is an American artist based in Brooklyn, New York.  Martin was born in Washington DC.  He makes abstract paintings with elements of collage which reference New Age and nature imagery.  Martin is influenced by artists such as Paul Feeley, Yayoi Kusama, Alfred Jensen and others who are also concerned with the formal qualities of painting.

Sarah Dollard | Sarah Dollard (born 21 January 1980) is an Australian screenwriter, living and working in the United Kingdom.  She made her start in writing for television on the long-running Australian soap opera "Neighbours", before moving to the UK in 2008.  Stints working in the script departments of British fantasy and science fiction TV shows, "Merlin" and "Primeval", led to writing episodes of BBC Three supernatural television series "Being Human" and British spy thriller "The Game", which first broadcast on BBC America.  She created and wrote the Welsh romantic comedy series "Cara Fi" (Love Me), which debuted on S4C in 2014.  She wrote the episode "Face the Raven" for Series 9 and the episode "Thin Ice" for Series 10 of Doctor Who.

The Copper Promise | The Copper Promise is a 2013 fantasy novella series by Jen Williams and her debut work.  It was initially released between December 2013 and January 2014 as a set of four e-book novellas and has since been published in paperback format as a single volume through Headline (ISBN  ).
response.summary_1: Passage [1]: "The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin" and "was reworked in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo." Passage [2]: "Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla." Bridge: the 2014 rework by Luis Royo links the Spanish artist (born in Olalla in 1954) to the novella The Ice Dragon; next hop should retrieve a source confirming the 2014 rework citation or directly verify the novella's authorship (George R. R. Martin) if needed.
response.hop2_query: The Ice Dragon 2014 Luis Royo illustrated edition George R. R. Martin
hop2_docs: The Ice Dragon | The Ice Dragon is a children's fantasy novella by George R. R. Martin, originally published in 1980 in the Ace Books anthology "Dragons of Light".  It was later included in Martin's 1987 collection "Portraits of His Children".  The story was reworked in 2007 with artwork by Yvonne Gilbert and again in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Luis Royo.

Luis Royo | Luis Royo (born 1954) is a Spanish artist born in Olalla, a village in the Aragonese province of Teruel.  He is best known for his fantasy illustrations published in numerous art books, magazines such as "Heavy Metal" and various other media including book and music CD covers, video games and Tarot cards.

The World of Ice & Fire | The World of Ice & Fire is a companion book for George R. R. Martin's "A Song of Ice and Fire" fantasy series.  Written by Martin, Elio M. García Jr. and Linda Antonsson, it was published by Bantam on October 28, 2014.  The 326-page volume is a fully illustrated "history compendium" of Martin's fictional Westeros, written from the perspective of an in-world "Maester" and featuring newly written material, family trees, and extensive maps and artwork.

A Dance with Dragons | A Dance with Dragons is the fifth of seven planned novels in the epic fantasy series "A Song of Ice and Fire" by American author George R. R. Martin.  In some areas the paperback edition was published in two parts titled "Dreams and Dust" and "After the Feast".  It was the first novel in the series to be published following the commencement of the HBO series adaptation, "Game of Thrones", and runs to 1,040 pages.

A Storm of Swords | A Storm of Swords is the third of seven planned novels in "A Song of Ice and Fire", a fantasy series by American author George R. R. Martin.  It was first published on August 8, 2000, in the United Kingdom, with a United States edition following in November 2000.  Its publication was preceded by a novella called "Path of the Dragon", which collects some of the Daenerys Targaryen chapters from the novel into a single book.

The Sons of the Dragon | The Sons of the Dragon is a novelette by George R. R. Martin.  It is set in the Westeros of Martin's "A Song of Ice and Fire" series, hundreds of years before the events of "A Game of Thrones" (1996) during the reigns of two Targaryen kings, Aenys I and Maegor I "The Cruel", both sons of the first Targaryen king, Aegon I "The Conqueror".  Following an early leak of the novella's inclusion in the anthology "The Book of Swords" by Martin's longtime friend Gardner Dozois, Martin himself confirmed that the anthology was scheduled to be released on October 10, 2017.

David A. Trampier | David A. Trampier (April 22, 1954 – March 24, 2014) was an artist and writer whose artwork for TSR, Inc. illustrated some of the earliest editions of the "Dungeons & Dragons" role-playing game.  Many of his illustrations, such as the cover of the original "Players Handbook", became iconic.  Trampier was also the creator of the "Wormy" comic strip that ran in "Dragon" magazine for several years.
response.summary_2: The fantasy novella was written by George R. R. Martin. It was reworked in 2014 with a series of original paintings by Spanish artist Luis Royo (born in Olalla in 1954) (see passages [1]–[2]).
